critic_raw:
The split strategy is temporally invalid for the stated use case. The description explicitly uses stratified random sampling across all 5 years of encounters as the test set, which allows the model to train on data from year 5 while predicting outcomes for patients from year 1. Hospital readmission prediction is a forward-looking problem; at deployment, the model will only see historical data. A random split violates this causal ordering and inflates apparent performance by allowing the model to learn from future care patterns, coding behavior, and case mix. This is especially consequential over a 5-year window where EHR systems, treatment protocols, and coding standards demonstrably change.

The selection of "patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization" as the study population introduces a systematic sampling bias. High-utilizer patients are inherently more likely to be readmitted, so the study population has an artificially elevated base rate and a skewed risk profile compared to the general discharged population. A model trained and evaluated on high-utilizers will not generalize to the broader population at discharge; its calibration will be wrong when applied to first-time or infrequent patients. The description frames this as a data quality choice but it is a population validity constraint that must be prominently stated as a limitation.

Preprocessing transformers — including imputation, scaling, and encoding — are fit on the entire dataset prior to splitting, consistent with a leakage pattern. The description frames this as preventing "instability caused by small training set sizes," but this rationale does not justify the leakage: in a 5-year dataset of 50,000+ records, the training split is never small enough to justify fitting scalers on test data. The imputed median values for lab results, derived from the full dataset, will reflect the test period's clinical distribution rather than the training period alone.
defender_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission experiment has a structural comparison that is fairer than critics typically acknowledge. The RNN and logistic regression receive the same static and index admission features, but only the RNN receives the temporal sequence of diagnoses, procedures, and lab values. This is not an information asymmetry that favors the RNN — it is the correct experimental design for testing whether temporal modeling of clinical trajectories adds predictive value over static summaries. If both models received identical features, you would be testing model architecture in a vacuum, not the question that matters clinically: does the history of care tell you something about readmission risk that the current admission alone cannot?

Focusing on patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization is a reasonable and defensible data choice for this task. These patients have richer temporal sequences that enable meaningful sequence modeling — patients with sparse prior encounter histories would generate uninformative sequences for the LSTM, introducing noise rather than signal. This is not selection bias in the pejorative sense; it is appropriate alignment between the data requirements of the model and the patient population where the model would realistically add value.

Macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric is defensible for the same reason it is used in any imbalanced binary classification setting: it enforces that the readmission class and non-readmission class contribute equally to the summary metric, preventing a model that flags everyone as high-risk from achieving trivially high recall. The model selection protocol — hyperparameter tuning on validation set, then test set used exactly once — follows correct ML practice and is explicitly stated.

The class weighting strategy (inverse class frequency) for the LSTM, with matched weighting for logistic regression, is the appropriate imbalance handling approach. Using the same weighting for both models ensures that imbalance handling is not confounded with architecture in the comparison.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on 5-year temporal data violates causal ordering — model can train on future encounters and predict past ones
High-utilizer population selection creates sampling bias; model will not generalize to general discharged population
Preprocessing transformers including imputation and scaling fit on entire dataset before splitting — leaks test-period clinical distribution into training
RNN receives richer temporal sequence features than LR receives static aggregates — feature asymmetry confounds architecture comparison
No holdout validation on a geographically or temporally distinct population to assess generalizability
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on 5-year temporal data violates causal ordering — model can train on future encounters and predict past ones
Preprocessing transformers including imputation and scaling fit on entire dataset before splitting — leaks test-period clinical distribution into training
No holdout validation on a geographically or temporally distinct population to assess generalizability
